Will renters rise up in Toronto’s election?,"First Up Plus: Who gets the final say on a listing price? ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ LIVING HERE By Amber Shortt Good morning. Here’s the latest on cottage country’s new normal, how renters could shape the Toronto election and who should get final say on a listing price. GOOD WEEK FOR... Homebuilders. A jump in sales shows buyers are trickling back to the GTA’s newly built home market. But, as BILD’s CEO says, now is not the time to call the market “anywhere near being balanced” — we still need more housing. Homebuyers. Bidding wars are back — but if you have the means and a strong stomach, the Star’s David Olive says it’s a good time to get into the market. Here's why this time is different. Dockside deskers. Elon Musk may still have a few fans, as hybrid workers in Ontario’s cottage country hail his satellite internet service as a “game changer.” It’s one factor in cottage country’s new normal. Where should you eat next? If you’re wondering where to eat as the city heats up, you need Food Crawl, the Star’s free food newsletter. Whether it’s a strip mall standout or a downtown gem, Food Crawl will make you an insider on the best eats in Toronto and beyond. Sign up for free here. BAD WEEK FOR... Caledon residents, who are caught in the middle of the Peel Region divorce. It’s meant to help speed up housing, but sorting out the details will likely be a headache. Indebted Canadians. Canadians have racked up more household debt than any other G7 country, with about three-quarters stemming from mortgages — and it will likely get worse. Young investors. Not surprisingly, a new Statistics Canada report shows the real estate investment market is dominated by boomers, with the number of homes investor-owned at 15 per cent. Here’s who else is investing in Canadian real estate. TRENDING During the last municipal election, a candidate knocked on my door and told my partner not to sell our house, listing all the reasons property values in our former city of York neighbourhood would be rising. And when my partner told him we were renting? “Oh,” he said. “That’s too bad.” I’d say I can’t blame him for being surprised. To most people, the idea of a renter conjures up an image of an undergrad student in a basement eating Kraft Dinner — not a family of four in a detached house, well, also eating Kraft Dinner (parents of three-year-olds, you get it). But not only is the number of renting households increasing, it’s also becoming more diverse — meaning renters could be a force in next month’s mayoral election. “It’s everyone — young professionals, families, seniors — and they’re all struggling because Toronto is not an affordable city to live in,” tenant advocate Douglas Kwan told the Star’s Victoria Gibson and May Warren this week. Tenants’ frustration is mounting over rising rents, threats of eviction and, for some, the lost dream of home ownership as prices rise again. Mayoral hopefuls this time around are waking up to the notion that the 48 per cent of Toronto households who pay rent instead of a mortgage could decide their fate. But as policies to increase housing affordability roll off candidates’ tongues, the big question may not be whether renters vote, but will they vote together? As University of Toronto professor Matti Siemiatycki told the Star, it’s not a foregone conclusion that renters will vote as a block, saying, “Whether it’s urban and suburban, or motorist and cyclist and pedestrian, there are all sorts of other dynamics that people see as important to their identity and interests.” Still, as the housing crisis deepens, any candidate who ignores renters’ concerns may just find tenants have no interest in giving them a four-year lease on the mayor’s seat. And that’s too bad. LOCATION, LOCATION,LOCATION NEWS FROM AROUND THE GTA AND BEYOND “Drive until you qualify” is pushing home hunters farther from the city and to places like Bradford, Ont., where this home doubled in price in a decade. The Residences of the Well at Front St. and Spadina Ave. can only be rented, not bought by investors. One renter, life coach Katia Stern, shares why it works for her. This Richmond Street East building now boasts Canada’s first residential air-to-water heat pump — technology that could make all condos carbon-free. Check it out. Remember that Kensington laneway home with a rooftop shower? It’s still for sale for a slightly lower asking price of just under $3 million. Soak it in. [Realtor.ca] Whitby’s heritage committee decided a home built in the late 1800s, with no discernible historical value, can be torn down for a new housing complex. [DurhamRegion.com] In Vienna’s Gemeindebauten (social housing), the proportion of tenants’ incomes that goes to rent is in the single digits. What can Toronto learn? [The New York Times] SHOULD I OVERRULE MY AGENT ON LIST PRICE? ADVICE FROM THE EXPERTS The Star talked to Property.ca broker Julian Kashani about how agents decide on a list price, and what happens when an agent and seller disagree. Here’s what he said: When suggesting a price to a client, I usually do a comparative market analysis to get a sense of what similar properties have recently sold for. I even take my client to other currently listed properties in their area and show them the pluses and minuses of a property compared to their own. If we do disagree on price, I advise we price it a little below what we think the property is worth but do an offer date — only accepting offers on one day — and see what the market will offer. This sales strategy is often done in hot sellers’ markets and when it is hard to price a property because it is unique. If the client wants to list at a higher price, thinking we can lower it later if needed, I explain they risk staying in the market too long, which could lead buyers to wonder if there’s something wrong with the property. An agent’s job is to give their client the best possible information, but at the end of the day it’s their property and the price is their choice. Have a burning real estate question of your own? Email us at livinghere@thestar.ca. Thanks for reading. Before you go, here’s a look inside the moated community of Toronto Islands. Living Here will be back in your inbox next week. It takes at least 72 hours for the investigation in this case and we strongly recommend you to verify your account at that time. This training program tackles these challenges by emphasizing best and practical practices relevant to real-world warehouse workflows, ensuring clarity and facilitating effective application for heightened efficiency. Learning Objectives: At the end of the session, participants will be able to: Identify key challenges in modern warehouse operations and their business impact. Course Content Module 1: Introduction to Realities of the Times Understand the evolving environment impacting warehouse processes. Identify the challenges of conventional warehouse practices. Recognize the financial impacts of inefficiencies and unplanned expenses. Module 2: Warehouse and Warehouse Management Differentiate between basic warehouse functions and comprehensive warehouse management. Learn about the complexities of modern warehouse management. Understand the importance of adapting management approaches to current realities. Module 3: Warehouse Workflow Analyze the dynamics of warehouse workflows. Identify critical issues at various stages of the workflow. Gain a diagnostic perspective on workflow optimization. Module 4: Principles of Warehouse Management Learn the core principles that drive effective warehouse management. Understand the factors influencing warehousing quality. Explore the foundational knowledge required for efficient warehouse operations. Module 5: Warehouse Management Process Identify key processes within warehouse operations. Determine when and how management interventions are needed. Understand the step-by-step approach to managing warehouse activities. Module 6: Ideal Warehouse Model Explore the components of a productive warehouse system. Understand how to apply a model to evolving business needs. Learn about the benefits of an ideal warehouse setup. Module 7: Warehouse Management Best Practices Discover practical best practices for warehouse operations. Learn how to implement these practices effectively. Understand the benefits of adopting best practices in warehousing. Module 8: Warehouse Management Problems Identify common issues leading to inefficiencies in warehousing. Explore strategies to address and resolve these problems. Understand the impact of these issues on overall warehouse performance. Module 9: Good Warehouse Housekeeping Learn the importance of maintaining a well-organized warehouse. Discover best practices for warehouse housekeeping. Understand how good housekeeping contributes to overall efficiency and safety. Techs rise, but Dow falls on day of Bush speech

Technology shares climbed Monday as Pres. Bush gave a speech outlining a plan for long-term growth.Tech investors also heard positive comments on Intel by C.S. First Boston, and snapped up shares in the chip, storage and computer server hardware sectors. CNET's Tech index rose 15.22 points, or 1.53 percent, to 1,005.33, and the tech-heavy Nasdaq composite index gained 9.04 points, or 0.65 percent, to 1,382.54.Amid news of a weakening dollar and an unexpected rise in business inventories in May, broader markets sank. Recovering some from early-day losses, the Dow Jones industrials finished by giving up 45.4 points, or 0.52 percent, to 8,639.1, and the S&P 500 slid 3.47 points, or 0.38 percent, to 917.92.
Will IBM's new ""Shark"" sink or swim?

IBM announced a new top-end ""Shark"" storage system Monday that could boost the company's prospects against entrenched competitors in the depressed storage market.Three years ago, IBM introduced its first Enterprise Storage System product, code-named Shark, in an attempt to rebuild a market it had almost totally ceded to rivals EMC and Hitachi Data Systems.
INTL BUSINESS MACHINES 71.00 2.59% New products help absorb AMD pain

Advanced Micro Devices turned out two new chips Monday, but its most anticipated release this week will be its second quarter results.As expected, AMD launched on Monday a new mobile Athlon XP 1800+ chip for notebooks. The company also announced Monday it has begun shipping MirrorBit, its newest flash memory product for storing data in cell phones and other devices.
ADVANCED MICRO DEV 9.25 1.65% Microsoft tries to steal Macworld thunder

Microsoft on Monday launched the first of several pre-emptive strikes against Apple Computer's Macworld trade show by making announcements about new technologies far ahead of their delivery to market.The strategic attack comes as tensions mount between Apple and Microsoft. During Macworld five years ago, the two companies announced a five-year technology agreement, whereby Microsoft committed to continued development of Office and Internet Explorer for the Mac.
Real-time stock quotes from CNET News.com Investor.
30-day free trial!C.S. First Boston expects an upbeat outlook from Intel in tech notes 7/15/02

Analyst Tim Mahon predicts the chip titan will report second-quarter results in-line with expectations, but offer better-than-expected guidance for the third quarter. Intel rival AMD's poor quarterly results stemmed more from company-specific issues than PC market troubles, Mahon says. And he points to other evidence of PC strength, such as Dell's positive comments about the current quarter. Mahon also expects Intel to speak confidently about its new 845G chipset, which he suggests could pressure the shares of graphics chip firms. Mahon reiterates a ""buy"" rating on Intel.
Visit the Brokerage CenterEMC appoints independent director to board

No. 1 data storage machine maker EMC Corp. on Monday said it named an outsider to its board of directors, bowing to pressure from shareholders who said the board had too many insiders.EMC said Gail Deegan, the former chief financial officer of Houghton Mifflin Co., will become the eighth member of its board, filling a seat left vacant since the fall. Deegan, 55, will also be a member of the audit committee.

Assunto: The 6 Best Ways To Easily Make Money on Swagbucks (paid out over $923 million until now)

Please if you have not signed up yet on Swagbucks it is better to sign up here<https://www.swagbucks.com/p/register?rb=147927097&rp=1> before starting the 6 Best Ways To Easily Make Money on Swagbucks, just to better understand all the methods and options on the Swagbucks website.
Here is a quick overview of Swagbucks<https://www.swagbucks.com/p/register?rb=147927097&rp=1> and reward sites in general if you are unfamiliar.

You can make money online by participating in a number of activities on these websites. Online purchasing, completing surveys, watching videos, playing games, and signing up for offers are a few typical methods.

With Swagbucks, you are rewarded with points known as SBs. Then, you can either use your SBs to obtain cash through PayPal or exchange them for gift cards to retailers like Amazon. Each SB is valued at around one cent.

While there are many other rewards sites like Swagbucks, few compare in terms of size, popularity, and reputation. In fact, Swagbucks has now paid out over $923 million<https://www.swagbucks.com/p/register?rb=147927097&rp=1> in rewards to its members since it was launched in 2008.
Here are the strategies that, in my opinion, work best if you want to earn SBs fast.
Another simple way to earn SBs is to shop online, especially for purchases you were already planning to make.
Although these deals are uncommon, cash-back rates can reach up to 20%
column2: at big stores, rates normally range between 2% and 10%.

column1: If you receive 5% cash back on average on $500 in purchases each month, you will have earned $25 in gift cards or $300 in gift cards over the course of a year.
To increase your Swagbucks earnings in this manner, you must:
* Buy only what you would have bought anyhow to avoid blowing your money trying to rack up points.
* Look at the merchants that Swagbucks is promoting because you’ll frequently find offers with discounts of over 10% there.
For earning SBs fast, signing up for different services comes in at a very close second place, just behind money makers.

For example, current offers allow you to earn SBs by signing up for Chime (a free online bank account), Rocket Money (a budget tracker with other money-saving tools), and Acorns (a micro-investing app).
Signing up for various services is second only to money producers in terms of how quickly one may earn SBs.
These sign-up bonuses are primarily for financial services.
For instance, current promotions let you earn SBs by signing up for Acorns, Rocket Money, and Chime (a free online bank account with other money-saving features) (a micro-investing app).
The advantage is that you typically don’t have to pay anything upfront, unlike with money makers.
By far, using so-called money makers is the fastest way to accumulate SBs.
With a money maker, you can truly make money by making an upfront purchase. Subscription services generate the majority of revenue.
As an example, you can sign up for Hulu/Disney+ for $12.99 through Swagbucks, and that deal could allow you to earn 1,800 SBs (valued at $18).
The best part of taking surveys is that they typically pay right away.
The preceding methods could result in a delay of a few days or weeks before your SBs are credited to your Swagbucks account.
But with surveys, you can log in today, take surveys, and see the points in your account shortly thereafter. The drawback is that taking surveys won’t get you rich.
First and foremost, it’s important to understand that not all surveys are created equally.
The surveys worth more than 200 SBs are the best, according to my research.
These opportunities are scarce. But it’s best to stick to these if you want to earn more money from surveys than the minimum wage.
To find the highest paying surveys, just pop into the “Answer” section of Swagbucks and look for the surveys that pay the most in the least amount of time.
With Swagbucks’ robust referral program, you may accrue points over the short- and long-term.
When your friends sign up and make 300 SBs in their first 30 days, you’ll receive 300 SBs.
The best thing is that for as long as your friends are members, you will also receive 10% of their SBs.

Be sure to encourage your friends to download the browser extension <https://www.swagbucks.com/lp-savings-button?cmp=695&cxid=swagbuttonref&rb=114269874&extRefCmp=1&extRb=114269874&lang=fr> as well. By doing this, you get an additional 100 SBs, increasing your total earned via signups to 400 SBs.
A more recent Swagbucks feature is in-store discounts. And based on the available deals, they provide some fantastic opportunities to make money off of regular purchases.
Like the online money makers mentioned previously in the post, there are even a few money-making opportunities where you can really earn more SBs than the item costs (or very close to it).

To find the best offers, head over to the “In-Store Deals” section and sort by cash-back amount. This gives you a list of the offers with the most potential. From there, try to find an item you were planning to purchase.
You must then upload your receipt to Swagbucks from there (this is where the Swagbucks app, which is available for both Android and iOS devices, comes in handy).

‘Modest Rainstorm’,"Forbes NewslettersPlus: Introducing The 2023 30 Under 30 Asia ADVERTISEMENTBECOME A MEMBER • Newsletters • MyForbesGood morning, Nintendo released its much-anticipated installment to the Legend of Zelda franchise, Tears of the Kingdom, less than a week ago—and the video gaming giant announced that it sold over 10 million copies in its first three days. It’s flirting with top all-time sales records, particularly noteworthy given the game only released on a single console: Nintendo’s Switch.Critics, reviewers and yours truly have been mesmerized by the game, with many already calling it a strong contender for Game of the Year. If you’re one of the more than 10 million, good luck out there in Hyrule, Sarah Whitmire Senior Editor, Newsletters Follow me on Twitter and Forbes.comBREAKING NEWSMontana Gov. Greg Gianforte AP/Bobby Caina Calvan, File Montana became the first state in the U.S. to ban TikTok yesterday, implementing a fine for app stores that offer it starting January 1. It’s unclear how the ban will be enforced, though it will not punish or fine individual TikTok users, and it’s likely to face legal challenges. Disgraced Theranos CEO Elizabeth Holmes will report to prison at the end of the month for her 11-year sentence for defrauding investors, after her request to stay out of prison while appealing her conviction was denied. Holmes will likely begin her sentence at a minimum-security prison in Bryan, Texas.BUSINESS + FINANCEMike Roffler, former CEO of First Republic AFP VIA GETTY IMAGESIn opening remarks to House lawmakers on Wednesday, former First Republic CEO Mike Roffler blamed other failed banks for “contaminating” his firm, along with the media, social media and “technological advancements” around digital banking. Rep. Brad Sherman (D-Calif.) called First Republic one of the “three worst-run banks in America,” saying it got knocked out by a “modest rainstorm.”Growing optimism in the tech industry, powered by artificial intelligence, drove gains on Wall Street yesterday. The Dow jumped more than 400 points, and seven tech heavyweights, led by Tesla, Nvidia and Amazon, tacked on a combined $123 billion in market cap. TECH + INNOVATIONThe manufacturing process behind semiconductors, a necessary component of computer chips, is messy from the environmental perspective—a typical plant can produce 10 million gallons of polluted wastewater daily. Water-tech startup Gradient said it reached a $1 billion valuation yesterday, and it expects revenue to double this year to nearly $200 million, signaling the fast-growing need for cleaning up and recycling industrial wastewater. The Securities and Exchange Commission could soon bring enforcement action against Coinbase, and it is likely to mirror moves the agency brought against rival crypto exchanges Bittrex and Kraken, Berenberg analysts said in a research report. WEALTH + ENTREPRENEURSHIPA judge ruled that prosecutors in the U.S. Virgin Islands can subpoena billionaire Elon Musk through Tesla as part of their civil lawsuit against JPMorgan Chase over the bank’s ties to Jeffrey Epstein. Attorneys had tried to subpoena Musk since April for communication records he has with Epstein—they believe Epstein “may have referred or attempted to refer (Musk) to JPMorgan.” MONEY + POLITICSDemocrats are openly raising concerns about President Joe Biden’s willingness to compromise on work requirements for welfare recipients as part of ongoing negotiations to raise the federal debt limit. Rep. Jared Golden (D-Maine) criticized the president for failing to negotiate with Republicans earlier, joining a growing coalition of progressives who are critical of stricter welfare requirements. TRENDS + EXPLAINERSGETTY IMAGESWork from home and other benefits may be at risk after the emergency declaration for Covid-19 expired last week—and it comes with some tax consequences. The dilemma for employers: cease Covid-19 related payments, accept that they might be taxable or see if they might be otherwise deductible. For workers, perks like home internet stipends and WFH computers hang in the balance. Daily Cover Story Meet The Forbes 30 Under 30 Asia Class Of 2023READ ARTICLETOPLINEWith global economic uncertainty on the rise and a significant drop in VC funding, young entrepreneurs in Asia-Pacific have faced a new set of challenges in the past year. But the current environment hasn’t killed innovation. Judging by this year’s cohort of 30 Under 30 Asia honorees, creativity has proven to be a valuable asset to survive—and thrive—amid tougher circumstances.With a total of $1.6 billion in VC funding raised by this year’s listees (compared to $3 billion last year), their valuations may not be as high but their impact is: from deploying AI to streamline tasks across industries to tackling financial literacy inclusion and revamping craftsmanship-based and adaptive fashion. These outstanding young individuals are driven by their determination and desire for positive change.WHY IT MATTERSTake the duo of Akshay Rampuria and Yashovardhan Poddar, who founded Openhouse in 2018: they saw limitations in the Indian education system, and launched what became a chain of after-school tutoring hubs that offer support in math and science, as well as extracurriculars like robotics and hip-hop dance. Openhouse raised $17 million on the idea that “offline” education—aided by technology but taking place in-person—is a more holistic approach to learning.MOREMeet The Young Female Investors On The 30 Under 30 Asia 2023 ListADVERTISEMENTFACTS AND COMMENTSThe World Meteorological Organization released its latest predictions on climate change, predicting humanity will cross the key threshold of 1.5C by 2027: 1.5CExperts believe global temperatures below this threshold can help avoid the worst impacts of climate change. 98%Chance that one of the next five years will be the hottest on record. ‘Uncharted territory’Health, food and water security, as well as the environment, will all be impacted, WHO warns.STRATEGY AND SUCCESSThe word “diverse” is often used as a shortcut when speaking about diversity, equity and inclusion, but the word has its limits—and DEI is a space where words matter. Diverse is an adjective that describes a group or community, but it is not descriptive of an individual. This piece offers a helpful rundown on DEI literacy. VIDEOWelfare Work Requirements Emerge As Central Issue In Debt Ceiling FightWatch nowGAMESQUIZA Michigan man was charged yesterday with allegedly stealing what well-known prop from a classic film—estimated to be worth $3.5 million? A.Lightsaber from the original ‘Star Wars’ (1977)B.Piano from ‘Casablanca’C.Dress from ‘My Fair Lady’D.Ruby slippers from ‘The Wizard of Oz’Check Your AnswerFRASE BY FORBESUnscramble today’s anagram to solve this hidden phrase!Scroll to the bottom of today’s newsletter for a hint.Play Now TODAY'S PHRASE ACROSS THE NEWSROOMForbes is launching a brand-new newsletter this week focused on the Future of Work. Penned by my colleague Jena McGregor, the Future of Work newsletter will feature headlines, insights and ideas about where the world of work is headed next. Sign-up instantly here so you don’t miss tomorrow’s first edition.BEYOND THE NEWSROOMThe 2023 Forbes Iconoclast Summit will convene the world’s most iconic visionaries, financial titans, pioneering technologists and key executives—including Mellody Hobson and Ray Dalio—for conversations on the most critical issues impacting our economy and markets today. Join us in New York City on June 12.Thanks for reading! Follow us on Twitter for by-the-minute updates on the latest business and financial news throughout the day. If you’re playing Frase by Forbes, today’s hint is “To be very expensive or costly.” Good luck! Memorial Day Sale: Save 50%Unlock the tools and connections you need to succeed. column2: what happens if the U.S. defaults
column3: Colorado river states agree on water sharing deal,"Plus, hear our best advice for making friends wherever you move. View this email online by Suzanne Nuyen May 23, 2023 Good morning. Peggy Whitson, who broke the record for time spent in space, is back for more than a week at the International Space Station. Here's what else we're following today. Lawmakers have nine days left to agree on a plan for the nation's debt limit and avoid default on its bills, according to Treasury officials. Though Monday's talks ended without a deal, President Biden called them ""productive."" Alex Brandon/AP 🎧 If the U.S. defaults, social security, taxpayer returns and military benefits could be affected as the government decides which bills to pay first. On Up First today, NPR's Scott Horsley says a default could damage the nation's future borrowing capabilities, similar to how one's credit score falls when bills aren't paid. TikTok is suing Montana after the state passed a law that takes effect next year banning the app. TikTok's lawyers allege the law violates free speech rights and call Montana's concerns over China accessing American data ""unfounded speculation.” 🎧 NPR's Bobby Allyn says the First Amendment has historically been a ""very strong legal shield,"" and legal experts tell him the app has a strong case. But if Montana's law is upheld, Allyn reports it could create a ""snowball effect,"" and more states could make copycat laws. Water managers in California, Arizona and Nevada have agreed on a proposal to reduce their water intake from the drought-stricken Colorado River to keep it from running dry in exchange for over a billion dollars in federal payments. The deal runs through the end of 2026. 🎧 Learning to live with less water will likely be ""very expensive and potentially very painful"" for these states, according to KUNC's Luke Runyon. He adds that the river's story broadens the discussion of adapting to climate change and gives the rest of the country a real-time look at future challenges. Newsletter continues after sponsor message Enlighten me Stan Honda/AFP via Getty Images Enlighten Me is a special series with NPR's Rachel Martin on in-depth conversations about the human condition. How do you move beyond hate and anger after a tragedy? Simran Jeet Singh says his anger after seven people were killed in 2012 in a mass shooting at a Sikh temple was ""corrosive."" 🎧 In a conversation with NPR's Rachel Martin, he discusses how Sikh philosophy helped him deal with what happened and see the inherent humanity in everyone. Listen to the interview or read it here.One of the biggest challenges of moving is making a new group of friends. Whether you're a college grad moving to a new city or have always been a nomad, we've got strategies for finding a sense of belonging wherever you are. 👋 Tell your loved ones you're moving and see if they can connect you with new people. 👋 Reach out to old friends in new cities. 👋 Find activities and make them routine. With time, you'll see some familiar faces and potential friends. 👋 Don't put too much pressure on yourself to make friends — focus on the connections. Listen to Life Kit's guide to making friends in new places here. Jenn Chávez/OPB Fidele Munyanganizi, a refugee from the Democratic Republic of Congo, created Outgrowing Hunger because he missed the food from his homeland. The project helps refugees grow their cultural crops. (via OPB)A TikToker deep cleaned a couch she found on the street, and it horrified fans. We talked to a bedbug expert about what it takes to truly get rid of the pests.Pittsburgh’s National Aviary’s researchers believe the Ivory-billed woodpecker, thought to be extinct, is still alive but endangered. (via WESA) Listen to your local NPR station. Visit NPR.org to hear live radio from KUNC (edit station). This newsletter was edited by Majd Al-Waheidi. Enjoying this newsletter? Forward to a friend! They can sign up here.Looking for more great content? Check out all of our newsletter offerings — including Music, Politics, Health and more! You received this message because you're subscribed to Up First emails. This email was sent by National Public Radio, Inc., 1111 North Capitol Street NE, Washington, DC 20002Unsubscribe | Privacy Policy",1,0

Case ID Number: FCU-065-617-349 In accordance with NCUA User Agreement, your account access will remain limited until the issue has been resolved. Unfortunately, if the access to your account remains limited for an extended period of time, it may result in further limitations or eventual account closure. We encourage you to take 3-5 minutes out of your online experience and update your personal account records as soon as possible to help avoid this. We thank you for your prompt attention to this matter. Please understand that this is a security measure intended to help protect you and your account. Sincerely, NCUA Account Review Department",1,1
